coredata.dc:title: A sensitive and stable visible-light-driven photoelectrochemical aptasensor for determination of oxytetracycline in tomato samples
coredata.dc:creator: You, Fuheng
Wei, Jie
Cheng, Yong
Wen, Zuorui
Ding, Caifeng
Guo, Yingshu
Wang, Kun
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) has attracted increasing attention worldwide due to the relationship between food safety and human health problems. In this work, a visible-light-driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) OTC aptasensor was constructed using Bi4VO8Cl/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs) nanohybrids as photoactive material and OTC aptamer as identification element. Owing to the well matched heterojunction of Bi4VO8Cl and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), the photogenerated electron-hole pairs could be separated effectively, so that the photocurrent intensity of as-prepared Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids was about 7 times higher than pure Bi4VO8Cl and had higher stability. The constructed “signal-off” PEC aptasensor realized OTC detection in tomato samples with excellent sensitivity, specificity and repeatability. The photocurrent decreased with the increase of OTC concentration in a range from 0.1 nM to 150 nM, and the detection limit was 0.03 nM (S/N = 3). The national standard method was used to compare with our method and the results were consistent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxytetracycline
Photoelectrochemical aptasensor
Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs
Tomato

Analytical performance of the PEC aptasensor
Selectivity and stability of the PEC aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XPS spectra of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids: (A) survey, (B) Bi 4f, (C) Cl 2p, (D) O 1s, (E) V 2p, (F) C 1s and (G) N 1s.
(A) XRD patterns of Bi4VO8Cl and Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs and (B) UV−vis diffuse reflectance spectra of Bi4VO8Cl and Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs.

(A) Photocurrent responses and (B) EIS spectra of ITO (a), Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO (b), aptamer/Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO (c) and OTC/aptamer/Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO (d). (C) Effects of the aptamer concentration and (D) incubation time of OTC with the aptamer on the photocurrent response of the aptasensor.

(A) Photocurrent responses of aptamer-Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs electrodes at different concentrations of OTC: 0 nM (a), 0. 1 nM (b), 1 nM (c), 10 nM (d), 20 nM (e), 50 nM (f), 80 nM (g), 100 nM (h), 120 nM (i), 150 nM (j) and 180 nM (k). (B) The corresponding linear calibration curve for OTC determination. (C) Selectivity and (D) stability of the PEC sensor based on Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs for OTC detection.
(A) Schematic illustration of PEC aptasensor for detecting OTC based on Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids and (B) PEC response mechanism of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO.
Comparison of different analytical methods for OTC detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: ECL: electrochemiluminescence; HPLC: high performance liquid chromatography.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of OTC spiked in the tomato.

head.ce:title: A sensitive and stable visible-light-driven photoelectrochemical aptasensor for determination of oxytetracycline in tomato samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) has attracted increasing attention worldwide due to the relationship between food safety and human health problems. In this work, a visible-light-driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) OTC aptasensor was constructed using Bi4VO8Cl/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs) nanohybrids as photoactive material and OTC aptamer as identification element. Owing to the well matched heterojunction of Bi4VO8Cl and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), the photogenerated electron-hole pairs could be separated effectively, so that the photocurrent intensity of as-prepared Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids was about 7 times higher than pure Bi4VO8Cl and had higher stability. The constructed “signal-off” PEC aptasensor realized OTC detection in tomato samples with excellent sensitivity, specificity and repeatability. The photocurrent decreased with the increase of OTC concentration in a range from 0.1 nM to 150 nM, and the detection limit was 0.03 nM (S/N = 3). The national standard method was used to compare with our method and the results were consistent.

ce:section.ce:para: Tomatoes contain lycopene, β-carotene, vitamin A, C and other nutrients, which not only have high nutritional value, but also help to prevent cancer and various diseases (Kelebek et al., 2017; Tomas et al., 2017). According to statistics, bacterial canker disease during the tomato growth caused 84% crop loss in Canada, and nearly $ 300,000 economic loss in Michigan a year (Sen et al., 2015). Oxytetracycline (OTC) as a pesticide to protect crops in agriculture, can effectively prevents tomato from being infected by bacteria and has been widely used in the field of tomato planting (Cao et al., 2018; Patrίcia et al., 2009). Remarkably, OTC residues in tomato fruits and processed by-products are usually detected. These excessive residues can also accumulate and transfer to food, soil and effluents, leading to antibiotic resistance and posing a threat to food safety and human health (Yan et al., 2015; Zou et al., 2016; Chiesa et al., 2018). Thus, the regulatory agencies setmaximum residue limits (MRL) to monitor OTC residues, such as 0.25 mg/kg for tomato in Brazil (Brasil, 1985); 0.35 mg/kg for peach and pear in USA (EPA, 2005).

At present, different traditional analytical methods are used to detect OTC, including chromatographic methods (Kim and Lee, 2017) and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Naik et al., 2017; Perez-Silva et al., 2012). Although these methods have high sensitivity and can handle multicomponent samples, they are usually limited in practical application due to the time-consuming, costly apparatus and professional requirement (Liu et al., 2018; Pan et al., 2018). Besides, fluorescence detection technology (Liu et al., 2020) is also difficult to meet the requirements due to the complex equipment and tedious software (Lin et al., 2007). In order to meet the requirements of simplicity, fast and low cost, some new methods have been applied to OTC detection. For instance, Huang’s group proposed an electrochemical luminescence (ECL) detection method (Chen et al., 2014) and Gu’s group constructed a simple colorimetry for OTC detection (Kim et al., 2010). Both of them have realized the rapid detection of OTC, but the linear range and sensitivity need to be further improved. Therefore, it is imperative to exploit detection methods with high sensitivity and excellent stability for OTC monitoring.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing, an emerging technique for sensing platforms that combines photochemistry and electrochemistry, has received extensive attention due to a series of advantages such as high sensitivity, fast response, low cost and simple equipment (Freeman et al., 2013; Tong et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2014). Aptamer-based sensors have been widely used in the detection of various targets such as metal ions, small molecules, organic molecules, proteins, and bacteria. For instance, Sun et al. developed a photoelectric aptasensor for Pb2+ detection, with the introduction of aptamer, the aptasensor showed high sensitivity and excellent selectivity (Niu et al., 2019). In the design of PEC sensor, the selection of a suitable photoactive material was particularly important, because efficient and stable signal output was the prerequisite to ensure excellent performance of the sensor. Bismuth-based materials as visible-light-responsive photocatalyst (Yan et al., 2016; Gan et al., 2013; Yan et al., 2013) have been widely researched due to the unique layered structure, appropriate band gap and non-toxicity (Wang et al., 2013; Cheng et al., 2014; Hong et al., 2018). Among them, Bi4VO8Cl, with a Sillén-Aurivillius perovskite phase, has the advantages of narrow band gap and can be responded to visible light, so it has been widely used in the field of photocatalytic degradation, water decomposition (Hu et al., 2014), and it has good application prospects in PEC detection. Fan’s group compounded Bi4VO8Cl with carbon nanotube, its photocatalytic strength increased obviously but the stability was still worse (Zhang et al., 2017). However, the stability of photoactive material is an important guarantee for the excellent performance of PEC sensor. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have attracted much attention in various photoelectrochemical applications due to their size-dependent and edge-sensitive photoluminescence properties. Compared with other photoelectrochemical materials, N-GQDs can be used as an ideal electronic medium because of the good electrical conductivity, biocompatibility and photochemical properties (Zhu et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2017).

Herein, Bi4VO8Cl and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were combined via one pot solvent thermal method. The prepared Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids could effectively inhibit the recombination of photo generated electron-hole pairs, which not only enhanced the stability of photocurrent, but also significantly improved the photocurrent to 7 times of the pure Bi4VO8Cl. This could help to improve the sensitivity or broaden the detection range of “signal-off” PEC sensor (Liu et al., 2020). Further, a visible-light-driven PEC aptasensor was fabricated for OTC detection by employing Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids as photoactive material. Combined with the “signal-off” mode, this constructed sensor displayed excellent sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility.

N-GQDs were prepared by an easy reflux approach according to our previous report (Yin et al., 2016); C6H17O7N3, NH4VO3, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and HNO3 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). 0.1 M phosphate-buﬀ ;ered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was used for all electrochemistry measurements as the supporting electrolyte. OTC, chloromycetin (CHL), ticarcillin (TC), ampicillin (AMP), sulfadimethoxine (SDM), kanamycin (KAN) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) were purchased from Sunshine Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). We purchased the aptamer: 5′-CGT-ACG-GAA-TTC-GCT-AGC-GGG-CGG-GGG-TGC-TGG-GGG-AAT-GGA-GTG-CTG-CGT-GCT-GCG-GGG-ATC-CGA-GCT−CCA-CGT-G-3′ against OTC by Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The aptamersolution was received by dissolving aptamer in the PBS (pH 7.0) and kept at 4 °C in dark. Standard OTC samples with various concentrations were also prepared in PBS. All other reagents were analytical reagent grade. The other materials and apparatus involved in the experimental procedure were depicted in Supporting Information. Nano pure water (ultrapure water) with resistivity higher than 18.2 MΩ cm (Direct-Q UV, Millipore) was used throughout this work.

Taking above N-GQDs as the precursor, the Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids were prepared by one pot solvent thermal method. Briefly, 3.64 g Bi(NO3)3‧5H2O and 0.65 g BiOCl were added to 40 mL of aqueous nitric acid solution (4.0 M) gradually to form solution A. Then, 0.29 g NH4VO3 was slowly added to NH3‧H2O (2 wt%, 20 mL) to make solution B. Mixed solution A with solution B, then added moderate amount of N-GQDs. Adjusting the pH of precursor solution to 3 by NH3‧H2O (2 wt%), and then transferred it to 100 mL stainless steel Teflon-lined for reaction at 160 °C for 20 h. After that, the teflon-lined was chilled to room temperature, the composites were treated with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol, then desiccated in an oven at 60 °C for future use.

The ITO was treated with NaOH (1.0 M) solution and ultrasonically cleaned with ultrapure water and ethanol, separately. 20 μL of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs (2 mg/mL) was dropped onto the ITO electrode surface (0.5 cm2 square) and dry at room temperature. After that, 20 μL of OTC aptamer (2 μM) was immobilized on the Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO electrode and incubated at 37 °C. The obtained aptamer/Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO electrode was rinsed to remove unabsorbed aptamers completely with ultrapure water. Then, the interface was covered with OTC solutions of different concentrations which were reacted for 40 min at 37 °C, followed by washing with ethanol and ultrapure water, separately. Finally, the photocurrent of the aptamer/Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO was directed at OTC detection.

The practical detection of tomato samples was studied by using the constructed sensor, the tomatoes were bought in local supermarket and processed as previous method (Mohamed et al., 2014). Briefly, 500 mg dried and crushed tomato was extracted with 8 mL of methanol/HCl (95:5, v/v). After stirring and centrifugation at 4500 r/min for 10 min to obtain supernatant. Continue to use 5 mL acetone and repeat the above steps. The supernatants were collected and dried under N2 at 40 °C. The residue was resuspended in 5 mL of methanol/ultrapure water (50:50, v/v) and defatted with 5 mL of hexane for three times. The rest of the mixture was dried under N2 at 40 °C. Finally, the treated liquid was diluted with PBS and spiked with 1, 10, 30 nM OTC standards.

To further elucidate the composition of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids samples, XPS analysis was carried out. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 1 A) obviously showed the presence of Bi, Cl, O, V, C and N ingredients all included in Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids. As shown in Fig. 1B, two obvious peaks of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 are located at 159.2 eV and 164.5 eV were belong to Bi4VO8Cl. However, when Bi4VO8Cl was combined with N-GQDs, the Bi 4f peaks of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids moved slightly to the high binding energy (Yang et al., 2013). As well, the high binding energy of Cl 2p (Fig. 1C) and O 1s (Fig. 1D) in the Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids also moved slightly compared with pure Bi4VO8Cl, the O 1s peaks at 529.9 eV and 532.4 eV can be distributed to the lattice oxygen in the sample and oxygen species adsorbed from the atmosphere (Zhang et al., 2017). This phenomenon indicates that Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrid system has the interaction between Bi4VO8Cl and N-GQDs. In Fig. 1E, the V 2p peaks at 516.8 eV and 524.4 eV indicating the existence of vanadium ions in pentavalence. Moreover, the binding energies of 284.5 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.2 eV were assigned to C 1s, which were pertain to CC, CN, CN bonding in N-GQDs (Fig. 1F). And the N 1s XPS spectrum showed two peaks at the binding energies of 401.9 eV and 406.7 eV in Fig. 1G, which were belonged to pyridinic and graphitic N of N-GQDs (Yin et al., 2016). The XPS results above further confirmed the successfully synthesis of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs.

Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) image was employed to characterize the morphology of the prepared samples. As shown in Fig. S1A, N-GQDs were randomly combined and doped on the surface of the Bi4VO8Cl. Furthermore, Bi4VO8Cl and Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, all the principal diffraction peaks in Bi4VO8Cl phase could be corresponding (Zhang et al., 2017). However, the diffraction peaks of N-GQDs were neglected by XRD, which may be due to the relative amount of N-GQDs was too less and the peak strength was too weak (Yin et al., 2016). Moreover, no more diffraction peaks could be discovered, which demonstrated the high-purity of the samples.

Diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) was aimed at investigate the light absorption capacity of Bi4VO8Cl and Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids. As showed in Fig. 2B, Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids exhibited mildly red shift compared to pure Bi4VO8Cl in the visible region. Moreover, the band gap energy decreased significantly for Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids compared with Bi4VO8Cl, the bandgaps of Bi4VO8Cl and Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids estimated to be 2.5 eV and 2.0 eV (Fig. S2). The enhanced absorption and narrowed bandgap were favorable to enhance the visible light activity of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids (Ge et al., 2019). The UV–vis results indicated that N-GQDs played a crucial role in Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids, resulting increased PEC performances compared to pure Bi4VO8Cl. In Fig. 3 A and B, the photocurrent intensity of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrid was about 7 times that of Bi4VO8Cl. At the same time, after under several on/off irradiation cycles for 600 s, the photocurrent response of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids was very stable without noticeable change, while the photocurrent of Bi4VO8Cl was significantly attenuated. These results proved that the introduction of N-GQDs could significantly improve the intensity and stability of photocurrent response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the PEC aptasensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 A showed the PEC intensity of the PEC aptasensor preparation. In visible light irradiation, the ITO electrodes showed weak photocurrent response (curve a), and Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO displayed a high photocurrent (curve b) attributed to the excellent photoelectric conversion efficiency of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs. However, the decreased photocurrent intensity (curve c) compared with Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO was due to the steric resistance of OTC adaptor modification on the electrode surface (Ge et al., 2018). The PEC performance is further degraded (curve d) after the aptasensor having specific combination with 50 nM OTC. This phenomenon demonstrated that the proposed PEC aptasensing platform can be used for quantitative detection of OTC.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) characterization of ITO electrode further proved the successful preparation of PEC aptasensor. As showed in Fig. 4B, the EIS response of modified electrode was related to every preparation procedure of PEC aptassensor electrode. The equivalent circuit (Fig. 4B, inset) used for fitting the impedance data, where R s, R et, Z w and Q represented electrolyte solution resistance, electron transfer resistance, warburg impedance and constant phase elements, respectively. ITO electrodes exhibited a charge-transfer resistance (R ct) of about 2000 Ω (curve a), far more than the resistance of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO (600 Ω, curve b). When the aptamer modified on the Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO electrode (curve c), the value of R ct raised from 600 Ω to 750 Ω, suggesting the aptamer was successfully modified on the Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids surface (Ge et al., 2018). At the presence of 50 nM OTC, the R ct raised prominently to 1000 Ω because of the specific combination between OTC and OTC aptamer (curve d). The compound change farther restrained the electron diversion, leading to the increased Rct (Hao et al., 2017). All these results have confirmed that the proposed PEC aptassensor sensor has been successfully prepared.

The construction process of PEC aptasensor for OTC detection was illustrated in Scheme 1 A. Due to the introduction of N-GQDs could effectively improve the charge transfer, Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids exhibited higher photocurrent intensity under visible light irradiation. After aptamers and OTC were fixed on the electrode surface, the steric resistance effect was enhanced, which significantly obstructed the electron transfer, resulting in the decrease of photocurrent (Yan et al., 2015). With the increased of OTC concentration, the photocurrent intensity reduced gradually, forming a “signal- off” PEC aptasensor. The PEC response mechanism of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO was shown in Scheme 1B. Both of Bi4VO8Cl and N-GQDs could be excited by visible light and produced photo induced electron. Then the electron-hole pairs were generated by the electron jumping from the valence band (VB) of Bi4VO8Cl and N-GQDs to their conduction bands (CB). Owing to the band energy positions of N-GQDs was more negative than that of Bi4VO8Cl, the photoelectrons can rapidly transfer from the CB (−0.8 eV) of N-GQDs to CB (0.49 eV) of Bi4VO8Cl, then injected into ITO to generate the photocurrent. Meanwhile, the holes at the VB (2.69 eV) of Bi4VO8Cl could jump back to the VB (1.5 eV) of N-GQDs. This band alignment could effectively prevented the recombination of electrons and holes, thus ensuring a more stable and efficient photocurrent output than the pure Bi4VO8Cl. The improved photocurrent could broaden the response range of detection, which was helpful to extend the linear range and improve the sensitivity of “signal-off” PEC aptasensor. The detailed discussion of energy level calculation can be found in the Supporting Information.

In order to acquire the optimal experimental conditions of PEC aptasensor for OTC detection, the content of the N-GQDs in Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids, the combination concentration of OTC aptamer and the incubation time of the aptamer were optimized. As showed in Fig. S3, as the weight ratio of N-GQDs in Bi4VO8Cl/ N-GQDs increased from 1% to 10%, the photocurrent signal increased first and then decreased gradually, and shown the optimal photoelectric conversion performance at 5%. This might be because too many N-GQDs were superimposed on the surface of Bi4VO8Cl, which will hinder the light absorption of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs (You et al., 2019). Thus, the PEC aptasensor was constructed by Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs (5%) nanohybrids in our work. In addition, the performance of aptasensor was significantly affected by the aptamer concentration and incubation time. As shown in Fig. 4C, with the increase of aptamer concentration from 0.5 to 3.0 μM, the photocurrent signal decreased gradually. However, when the concentration of the aptamer exceeded 2.5 μM, the intensity of the photocurrent was almost to the vertex. This phenomenon could be interpreted by the fact that the aptamer loaded on the electrode surface had reached saturation. As a result, the 2.5 μM aptamer concentration was chosen as the most suitable situation to improve the performance of PEC aptasensor. As displayed in Fig. 4D, the photocurrent decreased with the extension of incubation time. When reaching about 40 min, the photocurrent performance remains basically unchanged. The results show that the aptasensor on the electrode surface had been fully integrated into a saturated state. Therefore, 40 min was the most suitable incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the PEC aptasensor
Selectivity and stability of the PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Interference experiments were carried out on some common antibiotic (CHL, TC, AMP, SDM, KAN and CIP) interferences in order to inquire the selectivity of the PEC aptasensor. At the same experimental conditions, the photocurrent responses of 500 nM interferences and 50 nM OTC were measured. The signal of these interferences were much lower than that of OTC (Fig. 5C), indicating that the established PEC aptasensor had good selectivity, which was attributed to the excellent specific recognition ability between aptamers and OTC. In addition, stability was another necessary index to evaluate the performance of PEC sensor. As showed in Fig. 5D, the photocurrent signal was recorded under several on/off irradiation cycles more than 10 times for 600 s. The photocurrent intensity of Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs/ITO with 50 nM OTC was very steady without obvious fluctuation. Moreover, the photocurrent did not decrease significantly after the electrode was deposited at 4 °C for two weeks, proving the high stability of prepared PEC aptasensor.

In order to verify the feasibility of PEC aptasensor in the actual analysis, the OTC detection was implemented in tomato samples. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries were in the range of 96.4–104.0% with the RSD in the range of 4.46–9.28%. The experimental result showed that this method could be applied to the reliably determination of OTC in real samples. The concentrations of the target substances detected by the PEC and HPLC–MS/MS methods were close. The difference between PEC method and HPLC–MS/MS method was less than 10% in most cases, indicating that the accuracy of the established PEC method was equivalent to that of HPLC-MS/MS method (Dorokhin et al., 2011).

In summary, the Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids was prepared by one pot solvent thermal method. Due to the heterojunction of Bi4VO8Cl and N-GQDs could promote the effective separation of electron–hole pairs, more stable and efficient photocurrent signals were obtained. Further, the Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs with a wide range of visible light absorption and excellent PEC performance was used as the photoelectrode to anchor aptasensor. Based on the specific recognition of aptamer and target, a “signal-off” PEC aptasensor for high sensitivity and specificity detection of OTC was successfully constructed. The prepared sensor showed wide linear range and low detection limit, and has been successfully applied to the analysis of OTC in tomato samples. The results were similar to that of HPLC-MS/MS methods, indicating that the PEC aptasensor based on Bi4VO8Cl/N-GQDs nanohybrids provided a brand-new platform for high sensitive and selective detection in foodstuff analysis.

Fuheng You: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Jie Wei: Data curation. Yong Cheng: Software. Zuorui Wen: Formal analysis. Caifeng Ding: Conceptualization. Yingshu Guo: Investigation. Kun Wang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375050 and 21675066), the Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018-87), the Special Foundation of China Postdoctoral (2019TQ0127) and the Foundation of Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (No. SATM201807).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.122944.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
